text: The surgeon carefully uses a harmonic scalpel to dissect and remove dense adhesions surrounding the gallbladder, which are visible due to acute inflammation. The adhesions are meticulously teased apart, with attention to avoid damaging nearby delicate structures such as the intestines.
The gallbladder is visibly thickly adherent to the colon, omentum, and liver. Pus is observed coming from the perforated side of the gallbladder, revealing the cavity filled with pus.

The surgeon clears pus and purulent material from the surgical field, addressing the challenging gallbladder due to extensive adhesions and inflammation of the gallbladder wall. The gallbladder is visibly inflamed and adherent, complicating surgical maneuvers.

The surgeon attempts to identify the liver bed, noting the difficulty due to chronic inflammation. Using a fundus-first approach, the gallbladder fundus is carefully demarcated and separated from surrounding tissues, aiding in reaching the Calot's triangle.

The surgeon attempts to identify the neck of the gallbladder, located opposite the basilar sulcus, and begins separating adhesions around the cystic duct. Both the cystic duct and artery appear larger than usual. The surgeon carefully separates posterior relations to prevent damage to surrounding structures.

In cases of chronic inflammation, the surgeon encounters accessory blood vessels due to long-standing disease. A new port is placed in the left mid-clavicular line for better traction. The cystic duct is clamped and secured with a double V stapler.

The surgeon staples the cystic duct, securing it with double staples due to its unusual diameter. Cold scissors are used to excise the cystic duct, with visible pulsations indicating its location amidst inflamed tissue.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to separate the remaining portion of the gallbladder from the liver bed, following a fundus-first approach. The gallbladder is partially intrahepatic, requiring careful traction and counter-traction at a ninety-degree angle to the separation area. The staples securing the duct and artery are checked to ensure they are properly applied.
